did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), and Kohonen neural networks for skin cancer diagnosis (SCD). Specifically, it employs a Kohonen neural network for image segmentation, a CNN for feature extraction, and an ANN optimized by the Improved Gray Wolf Optimization (IGWO) algorithm for classification of skin lesions using the ISIC-2016 and ISIC-2017 databases.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents a new method for skin cancer diagnosis using a combination of optimization techniques and Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs). It details a proposed model involving image segmentation with a Kohonen neural network, feature extraction with CNN, and classification with an ANN optimized by Improved Gray Wolf Optimization (IGWO). The study includes implementation results on ISIC-2016 and ISIC-2017 databases, reporting specific accuracy metrics (97.09% and 95.17%) and comparing them with previous methods, which indicates original experimental research rather than a review.

title: Skin cancer diagnosis (SCD) using Artificial Neural Network (ANN) and Improved Gray Wolf Optimization (IGWO)
brief_summary: This study proposes a skin cancer diagnosis method combining image segmentation, feature extraction, and classification using Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) and optimization algorithms. The approach utilizes a Kohonen neural network and Greedy Search Algorithm for lesion segmentation, a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for feature extraction, and an ANN optimized by the Improved Gray Wolf Optimization (IGWO) algorithm for classification. The model was evaluated on the ISIC-2016 and ISIC-2017 databases using metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, and F-measure. The proposed method achieved average accuracies of 97.09% on ISIC-2016 and 95.17% on ISIC-2017. These results represent an improvement of at least 0.5% compared to previous methods and a 2.5% increase over standard CNN models. The study concludes that the IGWO-optimized ANN effectively enhances diagnosis accuracy despite higher computational requirements.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study performs a binary classification task distinguishing between melanoma and non-melanoma (normal) classes using the ISIC-2016 and ISIC-2017 databases. In ISIC-2016, there were 75 melanoma samples in the test set; in ISIC-2017, there were 117 melanoma samples in the test set.
The study classifies samples as either melanoma or non-melanoma (normal). In ISIC-2016, there were 304 normal samples in the test set; in ISIC-2017, there were 483 normal samples in the test set.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into melanoma and non-melanoma (normal) classes.
all_task_types.details: The paper performs a binary classification task to diagnose skin cancer (SC). The model classifies samples from the ISIC-2016 and ISIC-2017 databases into two discrete categories: melanoma and non-melanoma (normal). The study reports classification metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, F-measure, and confusion matrices (TP, TN, FP, FN). The primary model is an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) optimized by Improved Gray Wolf Optimization (IGWO) for this classification purpose, following feature extraction by a CNN and segmentation by a Kohonen neural network.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2016
datasets_used.description: A public database provided by the International Symposium on Biomedical Images (ISBI) in line with the challenge of skin lesion analysis for skin cancer diagnosis. It contains color images of skin lesions with effects such as blur, reflection, noise, hair strands, and thin veins.

use.training: The collection of training samples was used to form the proposed model.
use.testing: Test samples were used to evaluate the performance of the proposed method, reporting accuracy, precision, recall, and F-measure.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2017
datasets_used.description: A public database provided by the International Symposium on Biomedical Images (ISBI) in line with the challenge of skin lesion analysis for skin cancer diagnosis. It contains color images of skin lesions with effects such as blur, reflection, noise, hair strands, and thin veins.

use.training: The collection of training samples was used to form the proposed model.
use.testing: Test samples were used to evaluate the performance of the proposed method, reporting accuracy, precision, recall, and F-measure.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To calculate and report classification metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, F-Measure, sensitivity, specificity, Classification Success Index (CSI), and Matthews correlation coefficient (MCC) for evaluating the performance of the proposed method and comparing it with baseline methods (ANN+LM, ANN+GWO, CNN).
To compare the accuracy and other metrics of the proposed IGWO-ANN method against previous methods (InSiNet, ANN+LM, ANN+GWO) on ISIC-2016 and ISIC-2017 databases to demonstrate improvement.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using accuracy, precision, recall, F-measure, sensitivity, and specificity metrics derived from confusion matrices, with claims of improvement based on absolute percentage differences rather than statistical significance.

ISIC-2016.notes: The proposed method achieved 97.09% accuracy, classifying 295 True Negatives (TN), 9 False Positives (FP), 73 True Positives (TP), and 2 False Negatives (FN) out of 379 test samples. Sensitivity was 97.3% and Specificity was 97%. The method improved accuracy by at least 0.79% compared to other methods, with InSiNet having the closest performance. The proposed method increased diagnosis accuracy by 2.5% compared to CNN models using ANN with LM training function. It also increased accuracy by about one percent compared to ANN + GWO.

ISIC-2017.notes: The proposed method achieved 95.17% accuracy. Sensitivity was 98.3% and Specificity was 94.4%. The method improved accuracy by at least 0.5% compared to other methods. Compared to the closest method (InSiNet), it increased sensitivity by 0.9% and specificity by 0.4%. The proposed method increased diagnosis accuracy by 2.5% compared to CNN models using ANN with LM training function. It also increased accuracy by about one percent compared to ANN + GWO.

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid system comprising Kohonen Neural Network (segmentation), Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) (feature extraction), and Artificial Neural Network (ANN) (classification) optimized by Improved Gray Wolf Optimization (IGWO).

segmentation_model.name: Kohonen Neural Network
segmentation_model.details: Used for initial lesion region identification, improved by Greedy Search Algorithm (GSA).

feature_extractor.name: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)
feature_extractor.notes: Filter dimensions and counts determined experimentally. Fully connected layers reduce feature dimensions to prevent loss of useful features.

classifier.name: Artificial Neural Network (ANN)
classifier.input_neurons: Equal to number of features extracted by CNN (100)

training_details.loss_function: Cross entropy loss (for CNN training), Training error rate (for ANN optimization)

classification_or_output_head.details: Two output neurons separating healthy and diseased categories.

preprocessing_techniques: Removal of redundant information such as hair strands and thin blood vessels using a morphological closing operator (structuring elements of 10 pixels in 90 and 180 degrees directions)
Blur removal using an unsharp filter to correct artifacts introduced by the morphological closing operator
Image subtraction (pre-processed image minus filtered image) to create a high-resolution image free of arteries and hair strands
Resizing of cropped Region of Interest (ROI) images to 150x150 pixels to ensure uniform input dimensions for the CNN
conclusions: The proposed method, which combines a Kohonen neural network for segmentation, a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for feature extraction, and an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) optimized by Improved Gray Wolf Optimization (IGWO) for classification, effectively diagnoses skin cancer.
The use of the IGWO algorithm to optimize the structure and weight vector of the ANN increases diagnosis accuracy by at least 5% compared to standard configurations.
The proposed method achieved an average accuracy of 97.09% on the ISIC-2016 database and 95.17% on the ISIC-2017 database.
The proposed method improves accuracy by at least 0.5% compared to previous methods, including InSiNet, ANN+LM, and ANN+GWO.

The IGWO-optimized ANN demonstrates superior performance in sensitivity and specificity compared to baseline methods, with sensitivity and specificity of 97.3% and 97% on ISIC-2016, and 98.3% and 94.4% on ISIC-2017.
The proposed method correctly classified 368 out of 379 test samples in the ISIC-2016 database, resulting in only 11 classification errors.
The combination of Kohonen neural network and Greedy Search Algorithm (GSA) for image segmentation provides acceptable performance in detecting target regions with high accuracy.
The IGWO algorithm achieves better fitness and faster convergence than the basic GWO algorithm, resulting in a lower training error rate (< 0.007) for the ANN model.
important_remarks: The study is limited to binary classification (melanoma vs. normal/non-melanoma), ignoring other skin cancer types (e.g., BCC, SCC) and benign lesions, which limits clinical applicability for differential diagnosis.

The paper explicitly acknowledges that the proposed IGWO algorithm consumes significantly more memory and processing power compared to conventional algorithms due to the large population size required for optimization.
No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated solely on the ISIC-2016 and ISIC-2017 datasets, raising concerns about generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

The study does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for performance comparisons; improvements are claimed based on absolute percentage differences (e.g., 'at least 0.5%') without confidence intervals.

The paper notes that newer ISIC databases (2018-2020) were not used for testing due to their large volume, potentially limiting the assessment of the model's performance on more recent or diverse data distributions.

The segmentation step relies on a Kohonen neural network combined with a Greedy Search Algorithm, which may introduce bias if the segmentation fails to accurately isolate the lesion, affecting downstream feature extraction.

The claim of 'at least 5%' accuracy increase over standard configurations appears inconsistent with the reported results (e.g., 2.5% over CNN models), suggesting potential exaggeration or differing baseline definitions not fully clarified.
